question: 4. A lumberman wishes to cut down a tree whose trunk is cylindrical and whose material is uniform. He will cut a notch, the two sides of which will be planes intersecting at a dihedral angle \( \theta \) along a horizontal line through the axis of the cylinder. If \( \theta \) is given, show that the least volume of material is cut out when the plane bisecting the dihedral angle is horizontal.
solution:
First Solution. Suppose \( 0 \leq \alpha_{1}<\alpha_{2}<\alpha_{2}+\theta<\pi / 2 \); then the wedgeshaped solid between the planes at angles \( \alpha_{1} \) and \( \alpha_{1}+\theta \) is smaller than the wedge between \( \alpha_{2} \) and \( \alpha_{2}+\theta \), because a simple rotation of the former through an angle \( \alpha_{2}-\alpha_{1} \) makes it a proper subset of the latter.

Consider now any asymmetrical wedge of angle \( \theta \) with cross-section \( A O B \). If \( A \) and \( B \) are on the same side of the horizontal through \( O \), then the above argument shows that the wedge does not have minimal volume.

Suppose then that \( A \) is below the horizontal, and \( B \) above it. By symmetry we can assume that \( A O B \) lies below the symmetrical wedge \( S O T \) of angle \( \theta \), as shown. The wedge \( A O S \) is congruent by symmetry with the wedge \( A^{\prime} O T \), which is, in turn, larger than wedge \( B O T \) (as shown above). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text { wedge } A O B= & \text { wedge } A O S+\text { wedge } S O B \\
& >\text { wedge } S O B+\text { wedge } B O T \\
= & \text { wedge } S O T
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the symmetrical wedge is a strict minimum.
Second Solution. Let \( a \) be the radius of the cylindrical tree, and let \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) be the angles between the planes of the cut and the horizontal;
\[
\beta=\alpha+\theta .
\]

The volume of the wedge is
\[
\begin{aligned}
V & =\int_{0}^{a} 2 x(\tan \beta-\tan \alpha) \sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}} d x \\
& =A(\tan \beta-\tan \alpha)
\end{aligned}
\]
(It is easy, but unnecessary, to evaluate the integral; in fact, \( A=2 a^{3} / 3 \).) We seek to minimize \( V \) by choice of \( \alpha \). This is equivalent to minimizing
\[
W=\tan (\alpha+\theta)-\tan \alpha
\]
for \( -\pi / 2<\alpha<\pi / 2-\theta \), since \( -\pi / 2<\alpha \) and \( \beta<\pi / 2 \). The critical points are found by solving
\[
\frac{d W}{d \alpha}=\sec ^{2}(\alpha+\theta)-\sec ^{2} \alpha=0
\]

Since both \( \sec (\alpha+\theta) \) and \( \sec \alpha \) are positive through the interval in question, \( \sec (\alpha+\theta)=\sec \alpha \), whence \( \alpha+\theta= \pm \alpha \). Since \( \theta \) is not zero, the only critical point is given by \( \alpha=-\theta / 2 \). It is easily seen to correspond to a minimum. When \( \alpha=-\theta / 2, \beta=\theta / 2 \) and the horizontal plane bisects the wedge.

descriptive_long.question: 4. A lumberman wishes to cut down a tree whose trunk is cylindrical and whose material is uniform. He will cut a notch, the two sides of which will be planes intersecting at a dihedral angle \( notchangle \) along a horizontal line through the axis of the cylinder. If \( notchangle \) is given, show that the least volume of material is cut out when the plane bisecting the dihedral angle is horizontal.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Suppose \( 0 \leq angleone < angletwo < angletwo + notchangle < \pi / 2 \); then the wedge-shaped solid between the planes at angles \( angleone \) and \( angleone + notchangle \) is smaller than the wedge between \( angletwo \) and \( angletwo + notchangle \), because a simple rotation of the former through an angle \( angletwo - angleone \) makes it a proper subset of the latter.

Consider now any asymmetrical wedge of angle \( notchangle \) with cross-section \( wedgeconst\; centerpoint\; pointb \). If \( wedgeconst \) and \( pointb \) are on the same side of the horizontal through \( centerpoint \), then the above argument shows that the wedge does not have minimal volume.

Suppose then that \( wedgeconst \) is below the horizontal, and \( pointb \) above it. By symmetry we can assume that \( wedgeconst\; centerpoint\; pointb \) lies below the symmetrical wedge \( points\; centerpoint\; pointt \) of angle \( notchangle \), as shown. The wedge \( wedgeconst\; centerpoint\; points \) is congruent by symmetry with the wedge \( wedgeconst^{\prime}\; centerpoint\; pointt \), which is, in turn, larger than wedge \( pointb\; centerpoint\; pointt \) (as shown above). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{wedge } wedgeconst\; centerpoint\; pointb &= \text{wedge } wedgeconst\; centerpoint\; points + \text{wedge } points\; centerpoint\; pointb \\
&> \text{wedge } points\; centerpoint\; pointb + \text{wedge } pointb\; centerpoint\; pointt \\
&= \text{wedge } points\; centerpoint\; pointt
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the symmetrical wedge is a strict minimum.

Second Solution. Let \( treeradius \) be the radius of the cylindrical tree, and let \( generalangle \) and \( secondangle \) be the angles between the planes of the cut and the horizontal;
\[
secondangle = generalangle + notchangle .
\]

The volume of the wedge is
\[
\begin{aligned}
wedgevolume &= \int_{0}^{treeradius} 2\, axisdist (\tan secondangle - \tan generalangle) \sqrt{treeradius^{2} - axisdist^{2}}\, d axisdist \\
&= wedgeconst (\tan secondangle - \tan generalangle)
\end{aligned}
\]
(It is easy, but unnecessary, to evaluate the integral; in fact, \( wedgeconst = 2\, treeradius^{3} / 3 \).) We seek to minimize \( wedgevolume \) by choice of \( generalangle \). This is equivalent to minimizing
\[
meritfunc = \tan (generalangle + notchangle) - \tan generalangle
\]
for \( -\pi / 2 < generalangle < \pi / 2 - notchangle \), since \( -\pi / 2 < generalangle \) and \( secondangle < \pi / 2 \). The critical points are found by solving
\[
\frac{d\, meritfunc}{d\, generalangle} = \sec^{2}(generalangle + notchangle) - \sec^{2} generalangle = 0 .
\]

Since both \( \sec (generalangle + notchangle) \) and \( \sec generalangle \) are positive through the interval in question, \( \sec (generalangle + notchangle) = \sec generalangle \), whence \( generalangle + notchangle = \pm generalangle \). Since \( notchangle \) is not zero, the only critical point is given by \( generalangle = -\, notchangle / 2 \). It is easily seen to correspond to a minimum. When \( generalangle = -\, notchangle / 2, \; secondangle = notchangle / 2 \) and the horizontal plane bisects the wedge.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. A lumberman wishes to cut down a tree whose trunk is cylindrical and whose material is uniform. He will cut a notch, the two sides of which will be planes intersecting at a dihedral angle \( sunlight \) along a horizontal line through the axis of the cylinder. If \( sunlight \) is given, show that the least volume of material is cut out when the plane bisecting the dihedral angle is horizontal.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Suppose \( 0 \leq snowdrift<springtide<springtide+sunlight<\pi / 2 \); then the wedgeshaped solid between the planes at angles \( snowdrift \) and \( snowdrift+sunlight \) is smaller than the wedge between \( springtide \) and \( springtide+sunlight \), because a simple rotation of the former through an angle \( springtide-snowdrift \) makes it a proper subset of the latter.

Consider now any asymmetrical wedge of angle \( sunlight \) with cross-section \( sandstone\, pinecones\, rainstorm \). If \( sandstone \) and \( rainstorm \) are on the same side of the horizontal through \( pinecones \), then the above argument shows that the wedge does not have minimal volume.

Suppose then that \( sandstone \) is below the horizontal, and \( rainstorm \) above it. By symmetry we can assume that \( sandstone\, pinecones\, rainstorm \) lies below the symmetrical wedge \( driftwood\, pinecones\, goldcrest \) of angle \( sunlight \), as shown. The wedge \( sandstone\, pinecones\, driftwood \) is congruent by symmetry with the wedge \( sandstone^{\prime}\, pinecones\, goldcrest \), which is, in turn, larger than wedge \( rainstorm\, pinecones\, goldcrest \) (as shown above). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text { wedge } sandstone\, pinecones\, rainstorm= & \text { wedge } sandstone\, pinecones\, driftwood+\text { wedge } driftwood\, pinecones\, rainstorm \\ & >\text { wedge } driftwood\, pinecones\, rainstorm+\text { wedge } rainstorm\, pinecones\, goldcrest \\ = & \text { wedge } driftwood\, pinecones\, goldcrest
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the symmetrical wedge is a strict minimum.

Second Solution. Let \( velocity \) be the radius of the cylindrical tree, and let \( riverbank \) and \( moonlight \) be the angles between the planes of the cut and the horizontal;
\[
moonlight=riverbank+sunlight .
\]

The volume of the wedge is
\[
\begin{aligned}
meadowlark & =\int_{0}^{velocity} 2\, lanternfish(\tan moonlight-\tan riverbank) \sqrt{velocity^{2}-lanternfish^{2}} \, d\, lanternfish \\ & =sandstone(\tan moonlight-\tan riverbank)
\end{aligned}
\]
(It is easy, but unnecessary, to evaluate the integral; in fact, \( sandstone=2\, velocity^{3} / 3 \).) We seek to minimize \( meadowlark \) by choice of \( riverbank \). This is equivalent to minimizing
\[
buttercup=\tan (riverbank+sunlight)-\tan riverbank
\]
for \( -\pi / 2<riverbank<\pi / 2-sunlight \), since \( -\pi / 2<riverbank \) and \( moonlight<\pi / 2 \). The critical points are found by solving
\[
\frac{d buttercup}{d riverbank}=\sec ^{2}(riverbank+sunlight)-\sec ^{2} riverbank=0
\]

Since both \( \sec (riverbank+sunlight) \) and \( \sec riverbank \) are positive through the interval in question, \( \sec (riverbank+sunlight)=\sec riverbank \), whence \( riverbank+sunlight= \pm riverbank \). Since \( sunlight \) is not zero, the only critical point is given by \( riverbank=-sunlight / 2 \). It is easily seen to correspond to a minimum. When \( riverbank=-sunlight / 2, moonlight=sunlight / 2 \) and the horizontal plane bisects the wedge.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. A lumberman wishes to cut down a tree whose trunk is cylindrical and whose material is uniform. He will cut a notch, the two sides of which will be planes intersecting at a dihedral angle \( flatness \) along a horizontal line through the axis of the cylinder. If \( flatness \) is given, show that the least volume of material is cut out when the plane bisecting the dihedral angle is horizontal.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Suppose \( 0 \leq terminalangleone<terminalangletwo<terminalangletwo+flatness<\pi / 2 \); then the wedgeshaped solid between the planes at angles \( terminalangleone \) and \( terminalangleone+flatness \) is smaller than the wedge between \( terminalangletwo \) and \( terminalangletwo+flatness \), because a simple rotation of the former through an angle \( terminalangletwo-terminalangleone \) makes it a proper subset of the latter.

Consider now any asymmetrical wedge of angle \( flatness \) with cross-section \( perimeter edgepoint corepoint \). If \( perimeter \) and \( corepoint \) are on the same side of the horizontal through \( edgepoint \), then the above argument shows that the wedge does not have minimal volume.

Suppose then that \( perimeter \) is below the horizontal, and \( corepoint \) above it. By symmetry we can assume that \( perimeter edgepoint corepoint \) lies below the symmetrical wedge \( skewpoint edgepoint bentpoint \) of angle \( flatness \), as shown. The wedge \( perimeter edgepoint skewpoint \) is congruent by symmetry with the wedge \( perimeter^{\prime} edgepoint bentpoint \), which is, in turn, larger than wedge \( corepoint edgepoint bentpoint \) (as shown above). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text { wedge } perimeter edgepoint corepoint= & \text { wedge } perimeter edgepoint skewpoint+\text { wedge } skewpoint edgepoint corepoint \\
& >\text { wedge } skewpoint edgepoint corepoint+\text { wedge } corepoint edgepoint bentpoint \\
= & \text { wedge } skewpoint edgepoint bentpoint
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the symmetrical wedge is a strict minimum.
Second Solution. Let \( centerheight \) be the radius of the cylindrical tree, and let \( terminalangle \) and \( origindirection \) be the angles between the planes of the cut and the horizontal;
\[
origindirection=terminalangle+flatness .
\]

The volume of the wedge is
\[
\begin{aligned}
voidspace & =\int_{0}^{centerheight} 2 constantval(\tan origindirection-\tan terminalangle) \sqrt{centerheight^{2}-constantval^{2}} d constantval \\
& =perimeter(\tan origindirection-\tan terminalangle)
\end{aligned}
\]
(It is easy, but unnecessary, to evaluate the integral; in fact, \( perimeter=2 centerheight^{3} / 3 \).) We seek to minimize \( voidspace \) by choice of \( terminalangle \). This is equivalent to minimizing
\[
stillness=\tan (terminalangle+flatness)-\tan terminalangle
\]
for \( -\pi / 2<terminalangle<\pi / 2-flatness \), since \( -\pi / 2<terminalangle \) and \( origindirection<\pi / 2 \). The critical points are found by solving
\[
\frac{d stillness}{d terminalangle}=\sec ^{2}(terminalangle+flatness)-\sec ^{2} terminalangle=0
\]

Since both \( \sec (terminalangle+flatness) \) and \( \sec terminalangle \) are positive through the interval in question, \( \sec (terminalangle+flatness)=\sec terminalangle \), whence \( terminalangle+flatness= \pm terminalangle \). Since \( flatness \) is not zero, the only critical point is given by \( terminalangle=-flatness / 2 \). It is easily seen to correspond to a minimum. When \( terminalangle=-flatness / 2, origindirection=flatness / 2 \) and the horizontal plane bisects the wedge.

garbled_string.question: A lumberman wishes to cut down a tree whose trunk is cylindrical and whose material is uniform. He will cut a notch, the two sides of which will be planes intersecting at a dihedral angle \( pxncvhru \) along a horizontal line through the axis of the cylinder. If \( pxncvhru \) is given, show that the least volume of material is cut out when the plane bisecting the dihedral angle is horizontal.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Suppose \( 0 \leq cuvthspe<hxpqznor<hxpqznor+pxncvhru<\pi / 2 \); then the wedgeshaped solid between the planes at angles \( cuvthspe \) and \( cuvthspe+pxncvhru \) is smaller than the wedge between \( hxpqznor \) and \( hxpqznor+pxncvhru \), because a simple rotation of the former through an angle \( hxpqznor-cuvthspe \) makes it a proper subset of the latter.

Consider now any asymmetrical wedge of angle \( pxncvhru \) with cross-section \( sfrdlnke\, yxvmbtac\, wzcyrhqe \). If \( sfrdlnke \) and \( wzcyrhqe \) are on the same side of the horizontal through \( yxvmbtac \), then the above argument shows that the wedge does not have minimal volume.

Suppose then that \( sfrdlnke \) is below the horizontal, and \( wzcyrhqe \) above it. By symmetry we can assume that \( sfrdlnke\, yxvmbtac\, wzcyrhqe \) lies below the symmetrical wedge \( lgopnmsr\, yxvmbtac\, bravxkji \) of angle \( pxncvhru \), as shown. The wedge \( sfrdlnke\, yxvmbtac\, lgopnmsr \) is congruent by symmetry with the wedge \( sfrdlnke^{\prime}\, yxvmbtac\, bravxkji \), which is, in turn, larger than wedge \( wzcyrhqe\, yxvmbtac\, bravxkji \) (as shown above). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text { wedge } sfrdlnke\, yxvmbtac\, wzcyrhqe &= \text { wedge } sfrdlnke\, yxvmbtac\, lgopnmsr+\text { wedge } lgopnmsr\, yxvmbtac\, wzcyrhqe \\[-2pt]
& >\text { wedge } lgopnmsr\, yxvmbtac\, wzcyrhqe+\text { wedge } wzcyrhqe\, yxvmbtac\, bravxkji \\[-2pt]
& =\text { wedge } lgopnmsr\, yxvmbtac\, bravxkji
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the symmetrical wedge is a strict minimum.

Second Solution. Let \( lmrstqoi \) be the radius of the cylindrical tree, and let \( jdfgrmks \) and \( zekuanlp \) be the angles between the planes of the cut and the horizontal;
\[
zekuanlp=jdfgrmks+pxncvhru .
\]

The volume of the wedge is
\[
\begin{aligned}
hjgrksla &=\int_{0}^{lmrstqoi} 2 qzxwvtnp(\tan zekuanlp-\tan jdfgrmks) \sqrt{lmrstqoi^{2}-qzxwvtnp^{2}} d qzxwvtnp \\[-2pt]
& =sfrdlnke(\tan zekuanlp-\tan jdfgrmks)
\end{aligned}
\]
(It is easy, but unnecessary, to evaluate the integral; in fact, \( sfrdlnke=2 lmrstqoi^{3} / 3 \).) We seek to minimize \( hjgrksla \) by choice of \( jdfgrmks \). This is equivalent to minimizing
\[
nkpduolc=\tan (jdfgrmks+pxncvhru)-\tan jdfgrmks
\]
for \( -\pi / 2<jdfgrmks<\pi / 2-pxncvhru \), since \( -\pi / 2<jdfgrmks \) and \( zekuanlp<\pi / 2 \). The critical points are found by solving
\[
\frac{d nkpduolc}{d jdfgrmks}=\sec ^{2}(jdfgrmks+pxncvhru)-\sec ^{2} jdfgrmks=0
\]

Since both \( \sec (jdfgrmks+pxncvhru) \) and \( \sec jdfgrmks \) are positive through the interval in question, \( \sec (jdfgrmks+pxncvhru)=\sec jdfgrmks \), whence \( jdfgrmks+pxncvhru= \pm jdfgrmks \). Since \( pxncvhru \) is not zero, the only critical point is given by \( jdfgrmks=-pxncvhru / 2 \). It is easily seen to correspond to a minimum. When \( jdfgrmks=-pxncvhru / 2, zekuanlp=pxncvhru / 2 \) and the horizontal plane bisects the wedge.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 3$ and fix positive constants  

\[
R,\qquad \rho_{0},\qquad\text{and}\qquad \varphi\quad\text{with}\quad 0<\varphi<\pi .
\]

Write points of $\mathbb R^{n}$ in the form $x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n-1},z)$ with $z:=x_{n}$ the vertical
coordinate; the $z$-axis is  

\[
\Lambda:=\{(0,\dots ,0,z):z\in\mathbb R\}.
\]

The (right-circular) cylinder of radius $R$ about $\Lambda$ is  

\[
C:=\bigl\{x\in\mathbb R^{n}:x_{1}^{2}+\dots +x_{\,n-1}^{2}\le R^{2}\bigr\}. \tag{1}
\]

The material density depends only on the distance  

\[
r:=\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+\dots +x_{\,n-1}^{2}}
\]

from the axis and is given by  

\[
\rho(r)=\rho_{0}\bigl(1+(r/R)^{p}\bigr),\qquad \rho_{0}>0,\qquad p>-(n-1). \tag{2}
\]

(Finiteness of all integrals below is guaranteed by $p>-(n-1)$.)

Fix a horizontal unit vector $e$; without loss of generality take $e=e_{1}$.
For every real parameter  

\[
\alpha\in I_{\varphi}:=\bigl(-\pi/2+\varphi/2,\; \pi/2-\varphi/2\bigr) \tag{3}
\]

introduce the two non-vertical affine hyperplanes  

\[
P_{+}(\alpha):\; z=x_{1}\tan\bigl(\alpha+\varphi/2\bigr),\qquad
P_{-}(\alpha):\; z=x_{1}\tan\bigl(\alpha-\varphi/2\bigr). \tag{4}
\]

They intersect in the horizontal $(n-2)$-plane  

\[
L:=\{x_{1}=0,\; z=0\}. \tag{5}
\]

The dihedral angle between $P_{+}(\alpha)$ and $P_{-}(\alpha)$ equals $\varphi$, and for every admissible $\alpha$ the ``wedge'' inside $C$ cut out by the two planes is  

\[
W(\alpha):=C\cap\bigl\{\min \bigl(P_{+}(\alpha),P_{-}(\alpha)\bigr)
              \le z\le
              \max \bigl(P_{+}(\alpha),P_{-}(\alpha)\bigr)\bigr\}. \tag{6}
\]

(a)  Show that the total mass of $W(\alpha)$ is  

\[
M(\alpha)=K_{n,p}(R)\bigl[\tan(\alpha+\varphi/2)-\tan(\alpha-\varphi/2)\bigr], \tag{7}
\]

where  

\[
K_{n,p}(R):=\rho_{0}\,\omega_{n-2}\,R^{\,n}\,\frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}, \tag{8}
\]

and $\displaystyle\omega_{k}:=\pi^{k/2}\bigl/\Gamma(k/2+1)$ denotes the $k$-dimensional volume of the unit ball.

(b)  Prove that $M(\alpha)$ attains its unique global minimum precisely at $\alpha=0$; equivalently, the least mass is removed when the bisecting hyper-plane of the angle $\varphi$ is horizontal.

(c)  Compute this minimal mass explicitly:

\[
M_{\min}=2\,\rho_{0}\,\omega_{n-2}\,R^{\,n}\,
          \frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}\;\tan\bigl(\varphi/2\bigr). \tag{9}
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Geometry in the vertical $2$-plane  
Let $\Sigma:=\operatorname{span}\{e_{1},e_{n}\}$ with orthonormal coordinates $(x,z)$.  
The traces of $P_{-}(\alpha)$ and $P_{+}(\alpha)$ in $\Sigma$ are the lines  

\[
\ell_{-}: z=x\tan\bigl(\alpha-\varphi/2\bigr),\qquad
\ell_{+}: z=x\tan\bigl(\alpha+\varphi/2\bigr). \tag{10}
\]

Because $\alpha\in I_{\varphi}$ we have
$\tan(\alpha-\varphi/2)<\tan(\alpha+\varphi/2)$.
Hence, for any abscissa $x$, the vertical length of $W(\alpha)$ above that $x$ equals  

\[
\Delta z(x)=\lvert x\rvert\,T(\alpha),\qquad
T(\alpha):=\tan(\alpha+\varphi/2)-\tan(\alpha-\varphi/2)>0. \tag{11}
\]

Since $\lvert x\rvert\le R$, $W(\alpha)$ is bounded.

Step 2.  Mass element  
Fix $x\in[0,R]$ and write the remaining horizontal coordinates as $s=(x_{2},\dots ,x_{n-1})$.  
Set  

\[
B_{n-2}(x):=\bigl\{s\in\mathbb R^{\,n-2}:\lVert s\rVert^{2}\le R^{2}-x^{2}\bigr\}. \tag{12}
\]

Its $(n-2)$-dimensional volume is  

\[
\operatorname{vol}B_{n-2}(x)=\omega_{n-2}\,\bigl(R^{2}-x^{2}\bigr)^{(n-2)/2}. \tag{13}
\]

Over $B_{n-2}(x)$ erect a vertical prism of height $\Delta z(x)$.
Because $r^{2}=x^{2}+\lVert s\rVert^{2}$, the mass of this prism is  

\[
\mathrm dM(x)=\rho_{0}\,\Delta z(x)
              \int_{B_{n-2}(x)}
              \Bigl[1+\bigl(r/R\bigr)^{p}\Bigr]\mathrm ds
            =\rho_{0}T(\alpha)\,x\!
              \int_{B_{n-2}(x)}
              \Bigl[1+\bigl(r/R\bigr)^{p}\Bigr]\mathrm ds. \tag{14}
\]

Step 3.  Integration over the cross-section  
By symmetry in $x\mapsto -x$,  

\[
M(\alpha)=2\rho_{0}T(\alpha)
          \int_{0}^{R}x
          \int_{B_{n-2}(x)}
          \Bigl[1+\bigl(r/R\bigr)^{p}\Bigr]\mathrm ds\,\mathrm dx. \tag{15}
\]

Introduce cylindrical coordinates $(r,\theta)$ in the horizontal $(n-1)$-space:
$r\in[0,R]$, $\theta\in S^{\,n-2}$, with Jacobian
$r^{\,n-2}\mathrm dr\,\mathrm d\sigma_{n-2}(\theta)$.
Write $\theta_{1}:=\langle\theta,e_{1}\rangle=\cos\vartheta$.
The restriction $x\ge 0$ corresponds to $\cos\vartheta\ge 0$.
Equation (15) becomes  

\[
M(\alpha)=2\rho_{0}T(\alpha)
          \int_{0}^{R}r^{\,n-1}\Bigl[1+(r/R)^{p}\Bigr]\mathrm dr\;
          \int_{S^{\,n-2},\,\cos\vartheta\ge 0}\cos\vartheta\,
               \mathrm d\sigma_{n-2}(\theta). \tag{16}
\]

Angular integral.  
Because $\cos\vartheta$ depends only on $\vartheta$,

\[
\int_{\cos\vartheta\ge 0}\cos\vartheta\,
      \mathrm d\sigma_{n-2}(\theta)
      =\sigma_{n-3}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos\vartheta\,
                    \sin^{\,n-3}\vartheta\,\mathrm d\vartheta
      =\frac{\sigma_{n-3}}{n-2}
      =\omega_{n-2}. \tag{17}
\]

Radial integral.  With $u=r/R$,

\[
\int_{0}^{R}r^{\,n-1}\Bigl[1+(r/R)^{p}\Bigr]\mathrm dr
=R^{\,n}\int_{0}^{1}u^{\,n-1}\bigl(1+u^{p}\bigr)\mathrm du
=R^{\,n}\Bigl(\frac{1}{n}+\frac{1}{n+p}\Bigr)
=R^{\,n}\frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}. \tag{18}
\]

Combining (16)-(18) yields

\[
M(\alpha)=\rho_{0}\,\omega_{n-2}\,R^{\,n}\,
          \frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}\;T(\alpha), \tag{19}
\]

i.e. formula (7) with
$K_{n,p}(R)$ given by (8).  
Part (a) is proved.

Step 4.  Optimisation in $\alpha$  
Set  

\[
F(\alpha):=T(\alpha)=\tan(\alpha+\varphi/2)-\tan(\alpha-\varphi/2),
\qquad \alpha\in I_{\varphi}. \tag{20}
\]

Differentiation gives  

\[
F'(\alpha)=\sec^{2}(\alpha+\varphi/2)-\sec^{2}(\alpha-\varphi/2). \tag{21}
\]

A direct sign analysis avoiding the faulty claim from the draft solution:

Method 1 (parity argument).  
The function $g(t):=\sec^{2}t$ is even, continuous on $(-\pi/2,\pi/2)$ and
strictly increasing in $\lvert t\rvert$ because  

\[
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}g(t)=2\sec^{2}t\tan t,
\]
which has the same sign as $t$ in this interval.
Hence, for $\alpha>0$ we have
$\lvert \alpha+\varphi/2\rvert>\lvert \alpha-\varphi/2\rvert$ and therefore  
$g(\alpha+\varphi/2)>g(\alpha-\varphi/2)$, whence $F'(\alpha)>0$.
For $\alpha<0$ the inequalities reverse and $F'(\alpha)<0$.
Consequently  

\[
F'(\alpha)\begin{cases}
<0 &\text{if }\alpha<0,\\[2pt]
=0 &\text{if }\alpha=0,\\[2pt]
>0 &\text{if }\alpha>0.
\end{cases} \tag{22}
\]

Method 2 (integral representation, optional).  
Using the fundamental theorem of calculus,

\[
F'(\alpha)=\int_{\alpha-\varphi/2}^{\alpha+\varphi/2}
           2\sec^{2}t\tan t\,\mathrm dt .
\]

Because $2\sec^{2}t\tan t$ has the same sign as $t$, the integral is positive, zero, or negative according as $\alpha$ is positive, zero, or negative, giving the same conclusion.

Either way, $F$ is strictly decreasing on $(-\pi/2+\varphi/2,0]$
and strictly increasing on $[0,\pi/2-\varphi/2)$, so $\alpha=0$ is the unique global minimiser of $F$ and hence of $M(\alpha)=K_{n,p}(R)F(\alpha)$.
Part (b) is proved.

Step 5.  Minimal mass  
Insert $\alpha=0$ into (19):

\[
M_{\min}=K_{n,p}(R)\bigl[\tan(\varphi/2)-\tan(-\varphi/2)\bigr]
        =2\,K_{n,p}(R)\,\tan(\varphi/2). \tag{23}
\]

Using (8) gives (9). \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem is lifted from 3-space to ℝⁿ with n ≥ 3, which forces the solver to work with (n–2)-balls and general Beta/Gamma-function evaluations.
2. Variable density: Instead of uniform density, the mass depends on an arbitrary radial power r^p.  This introduces an additional parameter p and requires non-trivial averaging of the density over (n–2)-dimensional balls.
3. Multiple concepts: The solution combines classical geometry (dihedral angles and wedges), high-dimensional integration, special functions (Beta and Gamma), and monotonicity arguments for trigonometric functions.
4. Deeper computations: Determining the constant K_{n,p}(R) demands carrying out two coupled Beta–integrals and justifying the interchange of averaging and integration.
5. More subtle minimisation: Because of the extra density factor, one must show that the orientation part Δ(α) decouples from the radial integral, and then analyse Δ(α) carefully on its admissible domain.

All these layers render the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  3 and fix positive numbers  

  R, \rho _0, and \varphi  with 0 < \varphi  < \pi .  

Write points of \mathbb{R}^n in the form x = (x_1,\ldots ,x_{n-1},z) with z := x_n the vertical
coordinate; the vertical axis of the infinite right-circular cylinder  

  \Lambda  := {(0,\ldots ,0,z) : z\in \mathbb{R}}  

is the z-axis.  
The cylinder itself is  

  C := { x\in \mathbb{R}^n : x_1^2+\cdots +x_{n-1}^2 \leq  R^2 }.                             (1)

At every point the material has the radial density  

  \rho (r) = \rho _0 (1 + (r/R)^p),  r := \sqrt{x_1^2+\cdots +x_{n-1}^2},  \rho _0>0, p>-(n-1).   (2)

(Condition p>-(n-1) guarantees that all integrals occurring below are finite.)

Fix a horizontal unit vector e that we take, without loss of generality,
to be the first coordinate vector e_1.
For any real parameter  

  \alpha  \in  I_\varphi  := (-\pi /2 + \varphi /2,  \pi /2 - \varphi /2)                               (3)

define the two (non-vertical) affine hyperplanes

  P_+(\alpha ): z = x_1 tan(\alpha +\varphi /2),  
  P_-(\alpha ): z = x_1 tan(\alpha -\varphi /2).                                        (4)

P_+(\alpha ) and P_-(\alpha ) intersect in the horizontal (n-2)-plane  

  L := {x_1=0, z=0}.                                                 (5)

Their dihedral angle is \varphi , and for every admissible \alpha  the two planes bound
a bounded ``wedge'' inside C,

  W(\alpha ) := C \cap  { min(P_+(\alpha ),P_-(\alpha )) \leq  z \leq  max(P_+(\alpha ),P_-(\alpha )) }.        (6)

(a)  Show that the total mass of W(\alpha ) equals  

  M(\alpha ) = K_{n,p}(R) [ tan(\alpha +\varphi /2) - tan(\alpha -\varphi /2) ],                  (7)

where  

  K_{n,p}(R) := \rho _0 \omega _{n-2} R^{\,n} \cdot  (2n+p)/(n(n+p)),               (8)

and \omega _k := \pi ^{k/2}/\Gamma (k/2+1) is the k-dimensional volume of the unit ball.

(b)  Prove that M(\alpha ) attains its unique global minimum precisely at \alpha  = 0;
equivalently, the least mass is removed when the bisecting
hyper-plane of the dihedral angle is horizontal
(each cutting plane makes the same acute angle \varphi /2 with the horizontal).

(c)  Compute this minimal mass explicitly:

  M_min = 2 \rho _0 \omega _{n-2} R^{\,n} \cdot  (2n+p)/(n(n+p)) \cdot  tan(\varphi /2).       (9)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1. Geometry inside the vertical 2-plane \Sigma .

Let \Sigma  be the plane spanned by the vectors e_1 (horizontal) and e_n
(vertical).  In the orthonormal coordinates (x,z) on \Sigma  the two lines which
are the traces of P_-(\alpha ) and P_+(\alpha ) are

  \ell _- : z = x tan(\alpha -\varphi /2),        \ell _+ : z = x tan(\alpha +\varphi /2).         (10)

Because \alpha \in I_\varphi  the slopes satisfy tan(\alpha -\varphi /2)<tan(\alpha +\varphi /2); hence,
for every abscissa x the z-length of the section of W(\alpha ) lying above that
x equals

  \Delta z(x) = |x|\cdot T(\alpha ),  with  T(\alpha ) := tan(\alpha +\varphi /2) - tan(\alpha -\varphi /2)>0.   (11)

Consequently W(\alpha ) is bounded: even at |x|=R the height does not exceed
|R| T(\alpha ).

Step 2. Mass element.

Fix x with 0\leq x\leq R.
For the remaining n-2 horizontal directions introduce the vector
s=(x_2,\ldots ,x_{n-1}) and denote by

  B_{n-2}(x) := { s\in \mathbb{R}^{n-2} : \|s\|^2 \leq  R^2-x^2 }                    (12)

the (n-2)-ball of radius \sqrt{R^2-x^2}.
The (n-2)-dimensional Lebesgue measure of B_{n-2}(x) is

  vol B_{n-2}(x) = \omega _{n-2}(R^2-x^2)^{(n-2)/2}.                      (13)

For each point (x,s) the distance from the axis is
r=\sqrt{x^2+\|s\|^2}.  A vertical prism over B_{n-2}(x) of height \Delta z(x)
therefore carries the mass

  dM(x) = \rho _0 \Delta z(x)\int _{B_{n-2}(x)}[1+(r/R)^p]ds                    (14)

and, using (11) and (13),

  dM(x) = \rho _0 T(\alpha )\cdot x\int _{B_{n-2}(x)}[1+(r/R)^p]ds.                 (15)

Step 3. Integration over the whole cross-section.

Because the integrand only depends on |x| the total mass is twice the
integral over x\geq 0:

  M(\alpha )=2\rho _0T(\alpha )\int _{0}^{R}x
             \int _{B_{n-2}(x)}[1+(r/R)^p]ds dx.                      (16)

Introduce cylindrical coordinates in \mathbb{R}^{n-1}.
A point u=(x,s) is parameterised by its distance
r\in [0,R] from \Lambda  and a direction \theta \in S^{n-2}; write
\theta _1:=\langle \theta ,e_1\rangle =cos\vartheta  where \vartheta  is the angle with e_1.
The Jacobian is r^{\,n-2}dr d\sigma _{n-2}(\theta ), and x= r cos\vartheta .
Restricting to x\geq 0 amounts to keeping only directions with cos\vartheta \geq 0.
Hence (16) becomes

  M(\alpha )=2\rho _0T(\alpha )
         \int _{0}^{R}r^{\,n-1}[1+(r/R)^p]dr
         \int _{S^{n-2}, cos\vartheta \geq 0}cos\vartheta  d\sigma _{n-2}(\theta ).                    (17)

Angular integral.
Because cos\vartheta \geq 0 on the half-sphere {cos\vartheta \geq 0} and depends only on \vartheta ,

  \int _{cos\vartheta \geq 0}cos\vartheta  d\sigma _{n-2}(\theta )
         = \sigma _{n-3}\int _{0}^{\pi /2}cos\vartheta  sin^{\,n-3}\vartheta  d\vartheta 
         = \sigma _{n-3}/(n-2) = \omega _{n-2},                              (18)

where we used \sigma _{k}=2\pi ^{(k+1)/2}/\Gamma ((k+1)/2) and the identity
\sigma _{n-3}=(n-2)\omega _{n-2}.

Radial integral.  Substituting u=r/R,

  \int _{0}^{R}r^{\,n-1}[1+(r/R)^p]dr
         = R^{\,n}\int _{0}^{1}u^{\,n-1}[1+u^{p}]du
         = R^{\,n}\Bigl(\frac1n+\frac1{n+p}\Bigr)
         = R^{\,n}\frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}.                           (19)

Putting (18) and (19) into (17) yields

  M(\alpha )=\rho _0 \omega _{n-2} R^{\,n}\frac{2n+p}{n(n+p)}\cdot T(\alpha ).              (20)

By the definition (11) of T(\alpha ) this is exactly (7) with the constant
(8), completing part (a).

Step 4. Optimising the mass with respect to \alpha .

Set  

  F(\alpha ):=T(\alpha )=tan(\alpha +\varphi /2)-tan(\alpha -\varphi /2), \alpha \in I_\varphi .                     (21)

Differentiate:

  F'(\alpha )=sec^2(\alpha +\varphi /2)-sec^2(\alpha -\varphi /2).                                (22)

Because t\mapsto sec^2t is strictly increasing on (-\pi /2,\pi /2),

  F'(\alpha )<0 for \alpha <0, and F'(\alpha )>0 for \alpha >0.                         (23)

Hence F has a unique critical point at \alpha =0, which by (23) is the
global minimum on I_\varphi .  Since M(\alpha )=K_{n,p}(R) F(\alpha ) with K_{n,p}(R)>0,
the same conclusion holds for M.  This proves part (b).

Step 5. The minimal mass.

Insert \alpha =0 into (20):

  M_min = K_{n,p}(R)\cdot [tan(\varphi /2)-tan(-\varphi /2)]
           = 2 K_{n,p}(R) tan(\varphi /2).                               (24)

Using (8) gives the announced value (9), completing part (c). \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem is lifted from 3-space to ℝⁿ with n ≥ 3, which forces the solver to work with (n–2)-balls and general Beta/Gamma-function evaluations.
2. Variable density: Instead of uniform density, the mass depends on an arbitrary radial power r^p.  This introduces an additional parameter p and requires non-trivial averaging of the density over (n–2)-dimensional balls.
3. Multiple concepts: The solution combines classical geometry (dihedral angles and wedges), high-dimensional integration, special functions (Beta and Gamma), and monotonicity arguments for trigonometric functions.
4. Deeper computations: Determining the constant K_{n,p}(R) demands carrying out two coupled Beta–integrals and justifying the interchange of averaging and integration.
5. More subtle minimisation: Because of the extra density factor, one must show that the orientation part Δ(α) decouples from the radial integral, and then analyse Δ(α) carefully on its admissible domain.

All these layers render the enhanced variant substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.
